Model: GPT-5 | Size: 32k | Type: Code Assistant | Revision: 2025-05-28

### Diagnosis
- You’re in the STM32 ROM DFU (note: “DfuSe interface name: Internal Flash”), which cannot write to QSPI at 0x90040000.
- Your project is set to use the Daisy bootloader app type, which targets QSPI:

```6:10:/workspaces/WaveX/firmware/daisy/Makefile
# Use bootloader mode for larger programs (copies to SRAM on startup)
# This allows programs larger than 128KB flash limit
APP_TYPE = BOOT_SRAM
```

This makes `make program-dfu` try `-s 0x90040000`, causing: “Last page at 0x90054e5f is not writeable”.

### Fix options
- Option A (quick): Flash to internal flash (no Daisy bootloader).
- Option B (recommended): Install Daisy bootloader, then flash to QSPI.

### Option A — Flash to internal flash now
Run while the board is in ROM DFU mode (as you have now):
```bash
cd /workspaces/WaveX/firmware/daisy
make clean APP_TYPE=BOOT_NONE
make APP_TYPE=BOOT_NONE
make APP_TYPE=BOOT_NONE program-dfu
```

### Option B — Install Daisy bootloader, then use BOOT_SRAM
1) With the board in ROM DFU (“Internal Flash”):
```bash
cd /workspaces/WaveX/firmware/daisy
make program-boot
```
2) Power-cycle; ensure the Daisy bootloader grace-period LED pulse is visible (don’t hold the BOOT button).
3) Flash your app to QSPI via bootloader:
```bash
make clean APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM
make APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM
make APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM program-dfu
```

Notes:
- If you still see “Internal Flash” you’re in ROM DFU; for Daisy bootloader just reset normally without holding BOOT.
- The “Invalid DFU suffix signature” is a harmless warning.

- If you prefer using a debugger instead, set `APP_TYPE=BOOT_NONE` and program with ST-Link/OpenOCD via `make program`.

- Summary
- You were in ROM DFU, which can’t write QSPI; `APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM` forced QSPI address, causing the error.
- Either temporarily flash to internal flash (`APP_TYPE=BOOT_NONE`) or install the Daisy bootloader and then flash to QSPI.

Model: GPT-5 | Size: 32k | Type: Code Assistant | Revision: 2025-05-28

Status: I checked `firmware/daisy/Makefile` and confirmed `APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM`, which makes DFU target QSPI (0x90040000). Your device is currently in ROM DFU (“Internal Flash”), which can’t write QSPI. Best path: install the Daisy bootloader to internal flash, then continue using BOOT_SRAM/BOOT_QSPI to store the app in QSPI.

### Short answer
- Yes, having st-flash is helpful, but you don’t need it. The fastest fix is to install the Daisy bootloader via ROM DFU now, then continue using `APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM` and `make program-dfu`.

### Option 1 (recommended, no ST-Link required): Install Daisy bootloader via ROM DFU
Run these in `\`/workspaces/WaveX/firmware/daisy\`` while the device shows “@Internal Flash” in `dfu-util -l`:
```bash
make program-boot
```
Then power-cycle the board normally (don’t hold BOOT) to enter the Daisy bootloader (LED grace-period pulse). Build and program to QSPI:
```bash
make clean APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM
make APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM
make APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM program-dfu
```
Notes:
- BOOT_SRAM stores the image in QSPI and runs from SRAM (fast). BOOT_QSPI stores and executes from QSPI (slower, but pure XIP). Keep BOOT_SRAM unless you specifically want XIP.

### Option 2 (use ST-Link): Flash the bootloader with st-flash
If you prefer ST-Link:
```bash
cd /workspaces/WaveX/firmware/daisy
st-flash --reset write libs/libDaisy/core/dsy_bootloader_v6_3-intdfu-2000ms.bin 0x08000000
```
Then reset the board (normal boot to Daisy bootloader) and:
```bash
make clean APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM
make APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM
make APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM program-dfu
```

### Alternative (not for your end goal): Run without bootloader
If you just want a quick flash right now without bootloader:
```bash
make clean APP_TYPE=BOOT_NONE
make APP_TYPE=BOOT_NONE
st-flash --reset write build/wavex-daisy.bin 0x08000000
```
But this skips QSPI boot and doesn’t match your end goal.

If you want, I can run the bootloader install command (`make program-boot`) for you next.

- Installed plan: Use ROM DFU (or ST-Link) to flash `dsy_bootloader_v6_3-intdfu-2000ms.bin` at `0x08000000`, then keep `APP_TYPE=BOOT_SRAM` and use `make program-dfu` to program your app to QSPI.

---

Model: GPT-5 | Size: 32k | Type: Code Assistant | Revision: 2025-05-28

### Ways to monitor after flashing/reboot
- Bootloader DFU presence
  - If the Daisy bootloader is installed, you’ll see a brief LED pulse and a DFU device for ~2 seconds after reset. If it disappears, your app started; if it stays, the app didn’t boot.
  - Quick watch:
```bash
watch -n 0.3 'dfu-util -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "@Internal Flash" && echo "Bootloader (DFU)" || echo "App running"'
```

- USB device enumeration (if your app enables USB CDC/MIDI)
  - Logs:
```bash
dmesg -w
```
  - Serial (CDC):
```bash
ls /dev/ttyACM*  # should appear if CDC enabled
screen /dev/ttyACM0 115200
```

- ST-Link (you said you have st-flash/utils)
  - Start debug server:
```bash
cd /workspaces/WaveX/firmware/daisy
make openocd | cat
```
  - In a second shell, attach and run:
```bash
arm-none-eabi-gdb build/wavex-daisy.elf -ex "target extended localhost:3333" -ex "monitor reset halt" -ex "continue"
```
  - You can set breakpoints, inspect faults, and confirm the PC is running your app.

- Simple hardware heartbeat (most reliable)
  - Add a slow LED blink or GPIO toggle in your main loop to confirm the app is alive. Then just watch the LED.

- UART logging (if wired)
  - Use a 3.3V USB-UART on the UART your app configures (115200-8N1 typical), then:
```bash
screen /dev/ttyUSB0 115200
```

- Interpreting results
  - DFU visible only briefly → bootloader jumped to app (good).
  - DFU stays present → app not found/invalid; reflash via `make program-dfu`.
  - CDC/MIDI device enumerates → app USB stack is up (good).
  - GDB can continue from reset and hit your breakpoints → app executing (good).

- If you want, I can wire up a quick LED heartbeat in `src/main.cpp` so you have a visual “alive” indicator.
